subject: Physics
q: The magnetic field B crossing normally a square metallic plate of area 4 m2 is changing with time as shown in figure. The magnitude of induced emf in the plate during t=2 s to t=4 s, is _____ mV.
question: The magnetic field B crossing normally a square metallic plate of area 4 m2 is changing with time as shown in figure. The magnitude of induced emf in the plate during t=2 s to t=4 s, is _____ mV.
solution:
From the graph, it can be seen that the value of the magnetic field at t=2 s is 4 mT, whereas the magnetic field at t=4 s is 8 mT.

The formula to calculate the magnitude of the induced emf is given by

εind =dϕdt=ddtBA=AdBdt   ...1

dBdt is the slope of the given graph.

Hence, the value of the induced emf can be calculated as follows:

εind =4 m2×8 mT-4 mT4 s-2 s=8 mV

subject: Physics
q: A rectangular loop has a sliding connector PQ of length $\ell$ and resistance $\mathrm{R} \Omega$ and it is moving with a speed $v$ as shown. The set-up is placed in a uniform magnetic field going into the plane of the paper. The three currents $I_1, I_2$ and $I$ are
question: A rectangular loop has a sliding connector PQ of length $\ell$ and resistance $\mathrm{R} \Omega$ and it is moving with a speed $v$ as shown. The set-up is placed in a uniform magnetic field going into the plane of the paper. The three currents $I_1, I_2$ and $I$ are
solution: A moving conductor is equivalent to a battery of emf $=\mathrm{v} \mathrm{B} \ell \quad$ (motion emf) Equivalent circuit $$ \mathrm{I}=\mathrm{l}_1+\mathrm{l}_2 $$ applying Kirchoff's law $$ \begin{aligned} & \mathrm{I}_1 R+I R-v B \ell=0 \\ & \mathrm{I}_2 R+I R-v B \ell=0 \end{aligned} $$ adding (1) \& (2) $$ \begin{aligned} & 2 \mathrm{IR}+\mathrm{IR}=2 \mathrm{vB} \ell \\ & \mathrm{I}=\frac{2 \mathrm{vB} \ell}{3 \mathrm{R}} \\ & \mathrm{I}_1=\mathrm{I}_2=\frac{\mathrm{vB} \ell}{3 \mathrm{R}} \end{aligned} $$

subject: Physics
q: A square loop of side <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>7</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> is placed vertically in the east-west plane. A uniform magnetic field of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math> is set up across the plane in the north-east direction. The magnetic field is decreased to zero in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> at a steady rate. Then, the magnitude of induced emf is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mi>x</mi></msqrt><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is _______.
question: A square loop of side <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>7</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> is placed vertically in the east-west plane. A uniform magnetic field of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math> is set up across the plane in the north-east direction. The magnetic field is decreased to zero in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> at a steady rate. Then, the magnitude of induced emf is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mi>x</mi></msqrt><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is _______.
solution:
The area vector for the square loop is given by

A→=(0.1)2 j^ m2

According to the above diagram, the magnetic field is given by

B→=0.22i^+0.22j^

Hence, the magnitude of induced emf can be calculated as follows

e=ΔϕΔt=B→·A→-01=0.22i^+0.22j^·(0.1)2 j^ -01 V=2×10-3 V

Hence, x=2.

subject: Physics
q:
A square shaped coil of area 70 cm2 having 600 turns rotates in a magnetic field of 0.4 Wb m–2, about an axis which is parallel to one of the side of the coil and perpendicular to the direction of field. If the coil completes 500 revolution in a minute, the instantaneous emf when the plane of the coil is inclined at 60° with the field, will be ______ V.

(Take π=227)
question:
A square shaped coil of area 70 cm2 having 600 turns rotates in a magnetic field of 0.4 Wb m–2, about an axis which is parallel to one of the side of the coil and perpendicular to the direction of field. If the coil completes 500 revolution in a minute, the instantaneous emf when the plane of the coil is inclined at 60° with the field, will be ______ V.

(Take π=227)
solution:
Given here, N=600, A=70×10-4 m2, and B=0.4 T

Angular speed of coil is ω=2πn60=500×2π60=100π6 rad s-1

Instantaneous emf in coil is given by ε=-Ndϕdt, where, magnetic flux ϕ=BAcosωt and ωt=30° is angle between A and B.

So, ε=-NdBAcosωtdt=-N-BAωsinωt=NBAωsinωt

Putting the values, we get

ε=600×70×10-4×0.4×100π6×12

=44 V

subject: Physics
q: A rectangular loop of sides <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>, with its sides parallel to the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>-axis and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi></math>-axis respectively moves with a velocity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> in the positive <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> axis direction, in a space containing a variable magnetic field in the positive <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>z</mi></math> direction. The field has a gradient of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>cm</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> along the negative <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> direction and it is decreasing with time at the rate of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> If the resistance of the loop is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi>Ω</mi></math>, the power dissipated by the loop as heat is ______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>9</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
question: A rectangular loop of sides <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>, with its sides parallel to the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>-axis and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi></math>-axis respectively moves with a velocity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> in the positive <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> axis direction, in a space containing a variable magnetic field in the positive <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>z</mi></math> direction. The field has a gradient of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>cm</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> along the negative <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> direction and it is decreasing with time at the rate of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> If the resistance of the loop is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi>Ω</mi></math>, the power dissipated by the loop as heat is ______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>9</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
solution:
Let B0 is the magnetic field at origin. Then,

dBdx=-10-310-2

⇒∫B0BdB=-∫0x10-1dx

⇒B-B0=-10-1x

⇒B=B0-x10

Motional emf in AB=0

Motional emf in CD=0

Motional emf in AD=ε1=B0ℓv

Magnetic field on rod BC 

=B0--12×10-210

Motional emf in  BC=ε2=B0+12×10-210ℓ×v

Net equivalent emf εeq=ε2-ε1=300×10-7 V

For time variation

εeq'=AdBdt=60×10-7 V

εeqnet =εeq+εeq'=360×10-7 V

Power r=εeq net 2R=216×10-9 W

subject: Physics
q: $X P Q Y$ is a vertical smooth long loop having a total resistance $R$ where $P X$ is parallel to $Q Y$ and separation between them is $l$. A constant magnetic field $B$ perpendicular to the plane of the loop exists in the entire space. A rod $C D$ of length $L(L>l)$ and mass $m$ is made to slide down from rest under the gravity as shown in figure. The terminal speed acquired by the rod is $\_\_\_\_$ $\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}.(\mathrm{g}=$ acceleration due to gravity)
question: $X P Q Y$ is a vertical smooth long loop having a total resistance $R$ where $P X$ is parallel to $Q Y$ and separation between them is $l$. A constant magnetic field $B$ perpendicular to the plane of the loop exists in the entire space. A rod $C D$ of length $L(L>l)$ and mass $m$ is made to slide down from rest under the gravity as shown in figure. The terminal speed acquired by the rod is $\_\_\_\_$ $\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}.(\mathrm{g}=$ acceleration due to gravity)
solution:
As rod CD slides down with velocity $v$, it cuts magnetic field lines.
Induced EMF: $\varepsilon = BLv$
Induced current in the circuit: $I = \frac{BLv}{R}$
Magnetic force on the rod (upward, by Lenz's law):
$F_{mag} = BIL = \frac{B^2L^2v}{R}$
At terminal velocity, acceleration is zero.
Applying Newton's second law:
$mg - F_{mag} = 0$
$mg = \frac{B^2L^2v_{terminal}}{R}$
$v_{terminal} = \frac{mgR}{B^2L^2}$

subject: Physics
q: An inductor of inductance $10$ mH having resistance of $100\,\Omega$ is connected to battery of E.M.F. $1.0$ V through a switch as shown in the figure below. After switch is closed, the ratio of instantaneous voltages across the inductor when the current passing through it is $2$ mA and $4$ mA is _______.
question: An inductor of inductance $10$ mH having resistance of $100\,\Omega$ is connected to battery of E.M.F. $1.0$ V through a switch as shown in the figure below. After switch is closed, the ratio of instantaneous voltages across the inductor when the current passing through it is $2$ mA and $4$ mA is _______.
solution:
In an LR circuit, the equation for the voltage is given by Kirchhoff's loop law:

$E = L \dfrac{di}{dt} + iR$

The instantaneous voltage across the ideal inductor is the induced EMF, which is given by:

$V_L = L \dfrac{di}{dt} = E - iR$

Given values are:
$E = 1.0$ V
$R = 100\,\Omega$

For current $i_1 = 2$ mA $= 2 \times 10^{-3}$ A, the voltage across the inductor is:

$V_{L1} = 1.0 - (2 \times 10^{-3} \times 100) = 1.0 - 0.2 = 0.8$ V

For current $i_2 = 4$ mA $= 4 \times 10^{-3}$ A, the voltage across the inductor is:

$V_{L2} = 1.0 - (4 \times 10^{-3} \times 100) = 1.0 - 0.4 = 0.6$ V

The ratio of the instantaneous voltages is:

$\dfrac{V_{L1}}{V_{L2}} = \dfrac{0.8}{0.6} = \dfrac{4}{3}$

Answer: $4/3$

subject: Physics
q:
An ideal coil of $10 \mathrm{H}$ is connected in series with a resistance of $5 \Omega$ and a battery of $5 \mathrm{~V}$. 
$2$ second after the connection is made the current flowing in amperes in the circuit is
question:
An ideal coil of $10 \mathrm{H}$ is connected in series with a resistance of $5 \Omega$ and a battery of $5 \mathrm{~V}$. 
$2$ second after the connection is made the current flowing in amperes in the circuit is
solution:
$\mathrm{i}=\mathrm{i}_0\left(1-\mathrm{e}^{\frac{\mathrm{Rt}}{\mathrm{L}}}\right)$
$=\left(1-\mathrm{e}^{-1}\right)$

subject: Physics
q: A coil is placed in a time varying magnetic field. If the number of turns in the coil were to be halved and the radius of wire doubled, the electrical power dissipated due to the current induced in the coil would be (Assume the coil to be short circuited.)
question: A coil is placed in a time varying magnetic field. If the number of turns in the coil were to be halved and the radius of wire doubled, the electrical power dissipated due to the current induced in the coil would be (Assume the coil to be short circuited.)
solution:
\(\mathrm{P}=\frac{\varepsilon^2}{R}\)
where, \(\varepsilon=\) inducedemf \(=-\frac{d \phi}{d t}\)
\(\phi=\mathrm{NBA}\)
\(\varepsilon=\mathrm{NA} \frac{\mathrm{dB}}{\mathrm{dt}}\)
also, \(R \propto \frac{1}{\mathrm{r}^2}\)
where, \(\mathrm{R}=\) resistance and \(\mathrm{r}=\) radius of wire
\(\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{P} \propto \frac{\mathrm{N}^2 \mathrm{r}^2}{\mathrm{H}^2} \\ & \frac{\mathrm{P}_1}{\mathrm{P}_2}=1 \\ & \mathrm{P}_1=\mathrm{P}_2 \end{aligned}\)
This means if the number of turns were quadrupled and the wire radius halved, the electrical power dissipated would be remains same.

subject: Physics
q: A coil is suspended in a uniform magnetic field, with the plane of the coil parallel to the magnetic lines of force. When a current is passed through the coil it starts oscillating; it is very difficult to stop. But if an aluminium plate is placed near to the coil, it stops. This is due to :
question: A coil is suspended in a uniform magnetic field, with the plane of the coil parallel to the magnetic lines of force. When a current is passed through the coil it starts oscillating; it is very difficult to stop. But if an aluminium plate is placed near to the coil, it stops. This is due to :
options: development of air current when the plate is placed.
induction of electrical charge on the plate
shielding of magnetic lines of force as aluminium is a paramagnetic material.
electromagnetic induction in the aluminium plate giving rise to electromagnetic damping.
solution: Oscillating coil produces time variable magnetic field. It cause eddy current in the aluminium plate which causes anti-torque on the coil, due to which is stops.

subject: Physics
q:
The source of time varying magnetic field may be

(A) a permanent magnet

(B) an electric field changing linearly with time

(C) direct current

(D) a decelerating charge particle

(E) an antenna fed with a digital signal

Choose the correct answer from the options given below.
question:
The source of time varying magnetic field may be

(A) a permanent magnet

(B) an electric field changing linearly with time

(C) direct current

(D) a decelerating charge particle

(E) an antenna fed with a digital signal

Choose the correct answer from the options given below.
solution:
An electric field varying linearly with time will not produce variable magnetic field as current will be constant.

A decelerating charged particle can result in a time varying magnetic field by emitting radiation.

Similarly a permanent magnet has a constant magnetic field and a direct current does not change its direction and cannot produce a magnetic field.

An antenna can modulate the digital signals .

So, the right option is the statement D.

q: A bar magnet is passing through a conducting loop of radius R with velocity v. The radius of the bar magnet is such that it just passes through the loop. The induced e.m.f. in the loop can be represented by the approximate curve:
question: A bar magnet is passing through a conducting loop of radius R with velocity v. The radius of the bar magnet is such that it just passes through the loop. The induced e.m.f. in the loop can be represented by the approximate curve:
solution:
As Magnet is approaching toward the loop magnetic flux increases and Rate of increment of flux also increases so emf increases with time

 at this moment emf is maximum and negative

 Magnet is in middle of coil, at this moment emf is equal to zero

 Now again flux start changing however polarity of emf get reverse

 At this moment emf is again maximum

 emf again become zero

|ϵ|=dϕdt=20t+20 mV

|i|=|ϵ|R=10t+10 mA

At t=5

q: A boat is moving due east in a region where the earth's magnetic field is $5.0 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{NA}^{-1} \mathrm{~m}^{-1}$ due north and horizontal. The boat carries a vertical aerial $2 \mathrm{~m}$ long. If the speed of the boat is $1.50 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$, the magnitude of the induced emf in the wire of aerial is :
question: A boat is moving due east in a region where the earth's magnetic field is $5.0 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{NA}^{-1} \mathrm{~m}^{-1}$ due north and horizontal. The boat carries a vertical aerial $2 \mathrm{~m}$ long. If the speed of the boat is $1.50 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$, the magnitude of the induced emf in the wire of aerial is :
solution: $\mathrm{E}=\mathrm{B}_{\mathrm{H}} \mathrm{VV}=0.15 \mathrm{mV}$

subject: Physics
q: A coil of circular cross-section having 1000 turns and $4 \mathrm{~cm}^2$ face area is placed with its axis parallel to a magnetic field which decreases by $10^{-2} \mathrm{~Wb}$ $\mathrm{m}^{-2}$ in $0.01 \mathrm{~s}$. The e.m.f. induced in the coil is:
question: A coil of circular cross-section having 1000 turns and $4 \mathrm{~cm}^2$ face area is placed with its axis parallel to a magnetic field which decreases by $10^{-2} \mathrm{~Wb}$ $\mathrm{m}^{-2}$ in $0.01 \mathrm{~s}$. The e.m.f. induced in the coil is:
solution:
Given: No. of turns $N=1000$
Face area, $A=4 \mathrm{~cm}^2=4 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~m}^2$
Change in magnetic field,
$$
\Delta \mathrm{B}=10^{-2} \mathrm{wbm}^{-2}
$$
Time taken, $t=0.01 \mathrm{~s}=10^{-2} \mathrm{sec}$
Emf induced in the coil $e=$ ?
Applying formula,
Induced emf, $e=\frac{-d \phi}{d t}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
&=N\left(\frac{\Delta B}{\Delta t}\right) A \cos \theta \\
&=\frac{1000 \times 10^{-2} \times 4 \times 10^{-4}}{10^{-2}}=400 \mathrm{mV}
\end{aligned}
$$

question: In a coil, the current changes from $-2 \mathrm{~A}$ to $+2 \mathrm{~A}$ in $0.2 \mathrm{~s}$ and induces an emf of $0.1 \mathrm{~V}$. The self inductance of the coil is :

subject: Physics
q: Three identical coils $C_{1}, C_{2}$ and $C_{3}$ are closely placed such that they share a common axis. $C_{2}$ is exactly midway. $C_{1}$ carries current $I$ in anti-clockwise direction while $C_{3}$ carries current $I$ in clockwise direction. An induced current flows through $C_{2}$ will be in clockwise direction when
question: Three identical coils $C_{1}, C_{2}$ and $C_{3}$ are closely placed such that they share a common axis. $C_{2}$ is exactly midway. $C_{1}$ carries current $I$ in anti-clockwise direction while $C_{3}$ carries current $I$ in clockwise direction. An induced current flows through $C_{2}$ will be in clockwise direction when
options: $C_{1}$ moves away from $C_{2}$ and $C_{3}$ moves towards $C_{2}$
$C_{1}$ and $C_{3}$ move with equal speeds away from $C_{2}$
$C_{1}$ moves towards $C_{2}$ and $C_{3}$ moves away from $C_{2}$
$C_{1}$ and $C_{3}$ move with equal speeds towards $C_{2}$
solution:
Let the common axis be the z-axis.
$C_1$ is at $z = -d$, $C_2$ is at $z = 0$, and $C_3$ is at $z = +d$.
Current in $C_1$ is anti-clockwise (ACW), producing a magnetic field $B_1$ in the $+z$ direction at $C_2$.
Current in $C_3$ is clockwise (CW), producing a magnetic field $B_3$ in the $-z$ direction at $C_2$.
The net magnetic field at $C_2$ is $B_{net} = B_1 - B_3$. Since $C_1$ and $C_3$ are equidistant and carry equal current, initially $B_{net} = 0$.

To induce a clockwise current in $C_2$, Lenz's law states that the induced current must oppose the change in flux. A clockwise induced current produces a magnetic field in the $-z$ direction.
This happens if there is an increasing flux in the $+z$ direction or a decreasing flux in the $-z$ direction.

Case 1: $C_1$ moves towards $C_2$. The distance decreases, so $B_1$ (in $+z$ direction) increases. This causes an increasing flux in the $+z$ direction.

Case 2: $C_3$ moves away from $C_2$. The distance increases, so $B_3$ (in $-z$ direction) decreases. This causes a decreasing flux in the $-z$ direction.

Both actions (moving $C_1$ towards $C_2$ and moving $C_3$ away from $C_2$) result in a net change in flux that induces a clockwise current in $C_2$ to oppose the change.
Therefore, option (3) is correct.

subject: Physics

question: A rectangular metallic loop is moving out of a uniform magnetic field region to a field free region with a constant speed. When the loop is partially inside the magnate field, the plot of magnitude of induced emf $(\varepsilon)$ with time $(t)$ is given by
solution: $\begin{aligned} & E=-\frac{d \phi}{d t} \\ & \phi=B b x \\ & |E|=B b v\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: When the current changes from $+2 \mathrm{~A}$ to $-2 \mathrm{~A}$ in $0.05$ second, an e.m.f. of $8 \mathrm{~V}$ is induced in a coil. The coefficient of self-induction of the coil is
question: When the current changes from $+2 \mathrm{~A}$ to $-2 \mathrm{~A}$ in $0.05$ second, an e.m.f. of $8 \mathrm{~V}$ is induced in a coil. The coefficient of self-induction of the coil is
solution: $e=-\mathrm{L} \frac{\mathrm{dl}}{\mathrm{dt}} \Rightarrow 8=(\mathrm{L}) \frac{2-(-2)}{0.05} \Rightarrow \mathrm{L}=0.1 \mathrm{H}$

subject: Physics
q:
In the given circuit below inductance values of $L_1$, $L_2$ and $L_3$ are same. The magnetic energy stored in the entire circuit is $(U_t)$ and that stored in the $L_2$ inductor is $(U_l)$. $U_t / U_l$ is __________.
(Ignore the mutual inductance if any)
question:
In the given circuit below inductance values of $L_1$, $L_2$ and $L_3$ are same. The magnetic energy stored in the entire circuit is $(U_t)$ and that stored in the $L_2$ inductor is $(U_l)$. $U_t / U_l$ is __________.
(Ignore the mutual inductance if any)
solution:
Let the inductance of each inductor be $L$, so $L_1 = L_2 = L_3 = L$.

Let the total current flowing through the circuit be $I$. This current flows entirely through $L_1$.

Since $L_2$ and $L_3$ are connected in parallel and have equal inductances, the current $I$ divides equally between them. Therefore, the current through $L_2$ is $\dfrac{I}{2}$ and the current through $L_3$ is $\dfrac{I}{2}$.

The magnetic energy stored in an inductor is given by $U = \dfrac{1}{2} L i^2$.

The energy stored in the inductor $L_2$ is:
$U_l = \dfrac{1}{2} L \left(\dfrac{I}{2}\right)^2 = \dfrac{1}{8} L I^2$

The equivalent inductance of the entire circuit is:
$L_{eq} = L_1 + \dfrac{L_2 L_3}{L_2 + L_3} = L + \dfrac{L \cdot L}{L + L} = L + \dfrac{L}{2} = \dfrac{3L}{2}$

The total magnetic energy stored in the entire circuit is:
$U_t = \dfrac{1}{2} L_{eq} I^2 = \dfrac{1}{2} \left(\dfrac{3L}{2}\right) I^2 = \dfrac{3}{4} L I^2$

The ratio of the total energy to the energy stored in $L_2$ is:
$\dfrac{U_t}{U_l} = \dfrac{\dfrac{3}{4} L I^2}{\dfrac{1}{8} L I^2} = \dfrac{3}{4} \times 8 = 6$

Answer: $6$

subject: Physics
q:
A coil is placed in a magnetic field B→ as shown below :

A current is induced in the coil because B→ is :
question:
A coil is placed in a magnetic field B→ as shown below :

A current is induced in the coil because B→ is :
options: parallel to the plane of coil and increasing with time
parallel to the plane of coil and decreasing with time
solution:
Lenz's law state that the direction of the induced current is such that it opposes its cause of generation, so, 


B→, must not be parallel to the plane of coil for non-zero flux and according to Lenz's law if B is outward it should be decreasing for anticlockwise induced current.

subject: Physics
q: In the given figure, an inductor and resistor are connected in series with a battery of emf E volt. Ea2b J s-1 represents the maximum rate at which the energy is stored in the magnetic field (inductor). The numerical value of ba will be _____.
question: In the given figure, an inductor and resistor are connected in series with a battery of emf E volt. Ea2b J s-1 represents the maximum rate at which the energy is stored in the magnetic field (inductor). The numerical value of ba will be _____.
solution:
The formula to calculate the current flowing through an L-R circuit is given by

i=ER1-e-RtL   ...1

The energy stored within the inductor can be calculated as follows:

Einduction =12Li2=12LE2R21-e-RtL2   ...2

The rate of change of energy stored within the inductor is given by

ddtEinduction =21-e-RtL·e-RtL·RL·E2L2R2=E2Re-RtL-e-2RtL   ...3

For maximum rate,

d2Edt2=0   ...4

From equation (3) and (4), it can be written that

ddtE2Re-RtL-e-2RtL =0⇒e-RtL=2e-2RtL  ⇒eRtL=2   ...5

From equation (3) and (5), it can be written, under maximum energy stored condition for the circuit, that

ddtEinduction =E2R2-1-2-2=E24R

Substitute the values of the resistance and compare with the given expression to obtain the values of the parameters.

a=2b=2R=50

Hence, the required ratio is given by

ba=25

subject: Physics
q: The current in an inductor is given by $\mathrm{I}=(3 \mathrm{t}+8)$ where $\mathrm{t}$ is in second. The magnitude of induced emf produced in the inductor is $12 \mathrm{mV}$. The self-inductance of the inductor _____ $\mathrm{mH}$.
question: The current in an inductor is given by $\mathrm{I}=(3 \mathrm{t}+8)$ where $\mathrm{t}$ is in second. The magnitude of induced emf produced in the inductor is $12 \mathrm{mV}$. The self-inductance of the inductor _____ $\mathrm{mH}$.
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{I}=3 \mathrm{t}+8 \\ & \varepsilon=12 \mathrm{mV} \\ & |\varepsilon|=\mathrm{L}\left|\frac{\mathrm{dI}}{\mathrm{dt}}\right| \\ & 12=\mathrm{L} \times 3 \\ & \mathrm{~L}=4 \mathrm{mH}\end{aligned}$

q: A conducting circular loop is placed in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>Y</mi></math> plane in presence of magnetic field <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover accent="true"><mi>B</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mfenced><mrow><mn>3</mn><msup><mi>t</mi><mn>3</mn></msup><mover><mi>j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn><msup><mi>t</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mover><mi>k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced></math> in SI unit. If the radius of the loop is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>, the induced emf in the loop, at time, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi><mi>π</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> is _____ .
question: A conducting circular loop is placed in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>Y</mi></math> plane in presence of magnetic field <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover accent="true"><mi>B</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mfenced><mrow><mn>3</mn><msup><mi>t</mi><mn>3</mn></msup><mover><mi>j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn><msup><mi>t</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mover><mi>k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced></math> in SI unit. If the radius of the loop is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>, the induced emf in the loop, at time, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi><mi>π</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> is _____ .
solution:
By the faraday's law in electromagnetic induction

εind=-dϕdt=-dB→·A→dt

Magnetic flux is ϕ=B→·A→=3t3j^+3t2k^·π12k^

So, ϕ=3t2π

Now, induced emf is εind=dϕdt=6tπ

At t=2, εind=62π=12π V.

Thus, the value of n=12.

q: A rod of length $60 \mathrm{~cm}$ rotates with a uniform angular velocity $20 \mathrm{rad} \mathrm{s}^{-1}$ about its perpendicular bisector, in a uniform magnetic filed $0.5 T$. The direction of magnetic field is parallel to the axis of rotation. The potential difference between the two ends of the rod is _____V.
question: A rod of length $60 \mathrm{~cm}$ rotates with a uniform angular velocity $20 \mathrm{rad} \mathrm{s}^{-1}$ about its perpendicular bisector, in a uniform magnetic filed $0.5 T$. The direction of magnetic field is parallel to the axis of rotation. The potential difference between the two ends of the rod is _____V.
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \because \mathrm{V}_0-\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{A}}=\frac{\mathrm{B} \omega \ell^2}{2} \\ & \mathrm{~V}_0-\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{B}}=\frac{\mathrm{B} \omega \ell^2}{2} \\ & \therefore \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{A}}=\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{B}} \therefore \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{A}}-\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{B}}=0\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A $30$ cm long solenoid has $10$ turns per cm and area of $5$ cm$^2$. The current through the solenoid coil varies from $2$ A to $4$ A in $3.14$ s. The e.m.f. induced in the coil is $\alpha\times 10^{-5}$ V. The value $\alpha$ is ________.
question: A $30$ cm long solenoid has $10$ turns per cm and area of $5$ cm$^2$. The current through the solenoid coil varies from $2$ A to $4$ A in $3.14$ s. The e.m.f. induced in the coil is $\alpha\times 10^{-5}$ V. The value $\alpha$ is ________.
solution:
Given:
Length of the solenoid, $l = 30 \text{ cm} = 0.3 \text{ m}$
Number of turns per unit length, $n = 10 \text{ turns/cm} = 1000 \text{ turns/m}$
Area of cross-section, $A = 5 \text{ cm}^2 = 5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$
Change in current, $\Delta I = 4 \text{ A} - 2 \text{ A} = 2 \text{ A}$
Time interval, $\Delta t = 3.14 \text{ s}$

The self-inductance of the solenoid is given by:
$L = \mu_0 n^2 A l$

Substituting the given values:
$L = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times (1000)^2 \times 5 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.3$
$L = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 10^6 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-4}$
$L = 6\pi \times 10^{-5} \text{ H}$

The magnitude of the induced e.m.f. is:
$e = L \dfrac{\Delta I}{\Delta t}$

Substituting the values of $L$, $\Delta I$, and $\Delta t$:
$e = 6\pi \times 10^{-5} \times \dfrac{2}{3.14}$

Using $\pi \approx 3.14$:
$e = 6 \times 10^{-5} \times 2 = 12 \times 10^{-5} \text{ V}$

Comparing this with $e = \alpha \times 10^{-5} \text{ V}$, we get:
$\alpha = 12$

Answer: $12$

subject: Physics
q: In a series <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi><mi>R</mi></math> circuit <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>X</mi><mi>L</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mi>R</mi></math> and power factor of the circuit is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>P</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math>. When capacitor with capacitance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> such that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>X</mi><mi>L</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>X</mi><mi>C</mi></msub></math> is put in series, the power factor becomes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>P</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>. The ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msub><mi>P</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><msub><mi>P</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></mfrac></math> is
question: In a series <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi><mi>R</mi></math> circuit <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>X</mi><mi>L</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mi>R</mi></math> and power factor of the circuit is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>P</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math>. When capacitor with capacitance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> such that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>X</mi><mi>L</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>X</mi><mi>C</mi></msub></math> is put in series, the power factor becomes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>P</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>. The ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msub><mi>P</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><msub><mi>P</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></mfrac></math> is
solution:
In case of L-R circuit

Z=XL2+R2 Therefore, power factor

P1=cosϕ=RZ

As XL=R ⇒Z=2R

⇒P1=R2R⇒P1=12

In case of  L-C-R circuit

Z=R2+XL-XC2

According to the question XL=XC ⇒Z=R  ⇒P2=cosϕ=RR=1

⇒P1P2=12

subject: Physics
q: A conducting bar moves on two conducting rails as shown in the figure. A constant magnetic field B exists into the page. The bar starts to move from the vertex at time $t=0$ with a constant velocity. If the induced EMF is $\mathrm{E} \propto \mathrm{t}^{\mathrm{n}}$, then value of n is _.
question: A conducting bar moves on two conducting rails as shown in the figure. A constant magnetic field B exists into the page. The bar starts to move from the vertex at time $t=0$ with a constant velocity. If the induced EMF is $\mathrm{E} \propto \mathrm{t}^{\mathrm{n}}$, then value of n is _.
solution:
As the bar moves without change in orientation, the length of bar will be proportional to its distance from the vertex.
i.e. $I=c(v t)$
$\begin{aligned}
& \text { induced emf } E=B / v \\
& =c B v^2 t
\end{aligned}$
$\Rightarrow n=1$
